document_title: Acls And Classifier Policies Guide (For 832X, 93Xx, 100Xx Switch

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about About applying ACLs to VLANs?

About applying ACLs to VLANs: ACLs can be applied to VLANs only in the inbound (ingress) direction. Sequence numbering If no sequence number is specified, the software appends new ACEs to the end of the ACL with a sequence number equal to the highest ACE currently in the list plus 10. The sequence numbers may be resequenced using the access-list resequence command. Public ACL usage tips 12 ACL Logging Deny ACLs If multiple ACLs of different types are applied in the same direction, a deny ACE, whether explicit or implicit, in one ACL overrides a permit ACL in another. A deny ACE is an ACE within an ACL that uses the deny action keyword. Denied ping requests A ping request is denied when an ACL is applied on ingress or egress unless the request is explicitly permitted. switch# ping 100.1.2.10 PING 100.1.2.10 (100.1.2.10) 100(128) bytes of data.

document_title: Acls And Classifier Policies Guide (For 832X, 93Xx, 100Xx Switch
section: VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic?

VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic: NOTE This section applies to these AOS-CX platforms that support VXLAN and VXLAN ACLs: 5420, 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300/9300S, 10040, 10000. VLAN policies applied to an SVI will not match VXLAN traffic over a VNI. Interactions with VXLAN traffic on an L2 VNI About this task The way VLAN ACLs interact with VLAN traffic carried by VXLAN tunnels depends on the AOS-CX platform. Public About address and port object groups 14 For the purposes of this discussion, VLAN traffic is Ethernet traffic with an IP payload. VXLAN traffic includes a VXLAN header, encapsulating the Ethernet frame and IP header into the VXLAN UDP packet. VLAN traffic can be considered to be "normal" L2 traffic, and VXLAN traffic can be considered to be "tunneled" (encapsulated) L2 traffic.

topic: VLAN ACLs and interaction with VXLAN traffic

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the access-list secure-update command do?

The access-list secure-update command this command determines if access lists are updated using the secure-update feature. Secure-update is enabled by default. When secure update is enabled and an ACL is updated or replaced, one or more override entries are installed in the TCAM table(s) containing the ACL that is being modified. As a result, all traffic of the same type as the currently configured ACL will be denied on the interfaces to which the ACL is applied. This ensures that traffic is not temporarily allowed while modifying an ACL. Upon completion of the update, the TCAM override entries are uninstalled and traffic resumes ACL matching. The no version of this command disables this feature. If secure-update is disabled, there will be no override entry installed. Syntax: access-list secure-update.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to interface or LAG) command do?

The apply access-list (to interface or LAG) command applies an ACL to the interface (Individual front plane port) or Link Aggregation Group (LAG) identified by the current interface or LAG context. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the current interface or LAG identified by the current interface or LAG context. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6, or mac for MAC ACL. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the inbound (ingress) traffic direction. Selects the outbound (egress) traffic direction. Only for IPv4 AC Ls applied to route-only ports. Not available for ACLs applied Public apply access-list (to interface or LAG) 79 to IPv4 bridged ports, IPv6 ports, or MAC ACLs applied to ports. Syntax: no apply access-list {ip | IPv6 | mac} <ACL-NAME> {in | out}.

syntax: no apply access-list {ip | IPv6 | mac} <ACL-NAME> {in | out}

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to interface VLAN) command do?

The apply access-list (to interface VLAN) command applies an ACL to the interface VLAN (or range of interface VLANs) identified by the current interface VLAN context. Using the apply access-list command on an interface VLAN interface with an already-applied ACL of the same direction and type will replace the currently-applied ACL. The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the interface VLAN (or range of interface VLANs) identified by the current interface VLAN context. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the routed inbound (routed ingress) traffic direction. Selects the routed outbound (routed egress) traffic direction. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6} <ACL-NAME> {routed-in|routed-out}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply access-list (to subinterface) command do?

The apply access-list (to subinterface) command applies an ACL to the current port or LAGsubinterface context or subinterface context range. Public apply access-list (to subinterface) 84 The no form of this command removes application of the ACL from the current port or LAGsubinterface context or subinterface context range. NOTE An ACL cannot be applied to the parent interface of one or more subinterfaces. This also means that a subinterface cannot be added to an interface if there is an ACL applied. NOTE ACE VLAN IDs cannot be added to ACLs applied to subinterfaces. This also means that an ACL with an ACE matching on a VLAN ID cannot be applied to a subinterface. Specifies the ACL type: ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6, or mac for M AC ACL. Specifies the ACL name. Selects the traffic direction. Syntax: apply access-list {ip|IPv6|mac} <ACL-NAME> {in|out}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the object-group all reset command do?

The object-group all reset command resets the user configuration back to the active configuration for all object types (address and port). This command takes immediate effect, it is not saved in the user configuration. Use this command if misconfiguration of address object groups and port object groups has occurred. Individual address and port object groups can be reset respectively with the object-group address reset and object-group port reset commands. Syntax: object-group all reset.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the policy command do?

The policy command creates or modifies classifier policy and policy entries. A policy is made up of one or more policy entries ordered and prioritized by sequence numbers. Each entry has an IPv4/IPv6/MAC class and zero or more policy actions associated with it. A policy must be applied using the apply command. The no form of the command can be used to delete either a policy (use no with the policy command) or an individual policy entry (use no with the sequence number). Specifies the name of the policy. <POLICY-NAME> <SEQUENCE-NUMBER> Specifies a sequence number for the policy entry. Optional. Ran ge: 1 to 4294967295. Public policy 197 Stores the remaining entered text as a policy entry comment. class {ip|IPv6|mac} <CLASS- NAME> Specifies a type of class, ip for IPv4, IPv6 for IPv6 and mac for a MAC policy. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME>.

section: ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what restrictions or notes are documented for ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations?

For ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: Switches have finite (TCAM and other) hardware resources used in the application of ACLs and Classifier policies to packets being processed in switch hardware. ADC (analytics data collection) also consumes TCAM lookups. Take the considerations described in this chapter into account when deciding what ACL and classifier policy-related features to use at the same time. Subtopics Public.

topic: ACL and Policy hardware resource considerations

document_title: Acls And Classifier Policies Guide (For 832X, 93Xx, 100Xx Switch
section: ACL and Policy hardware resource commands

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about ACL and Policy hardware resource commands?

ACL and Policy hardware resource commands: Show Resources The show resources command allows users to see hardware resource consumption in the switch. These hardware resources include the following: • TCAM entries • Policers • High-Capacity TCAM entries On the 4100i, 6000, 6100, 8320, 8325, and 8360 switch series, these resources are reported for the entire switch. On the 9300 switch series, resources are reported per pipe. The 9300 switch series has four pipes and the 9300s and 10040 switch series has two pipes. The usage of high capacity TCAM can be viewed specifically using show system high-capacity-tcam. This command displays the feature that is currently configured, pending configuration, and the default feature present in high capacity TCAM.

topic: ACL and Policy hardware resource commands

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Ingress VLAN MAC ACL?

Ingress VLAN MAC ACL: • Ingress VXLAN Relay The following features use two slices when enabled: • Ingress L3 RX Statistics • Ingress Port MAC ACL The following features use three slices when enabled. Features denoted by an * can share ingress TCAM slices if they are IPv4 and IPv6 features of the same application type. In the show resources output, they display as separate features with an * in the Reserved entries column to denote that an IPv4 and an IPv6 feature of the same application type are sharing TCAM slices. • Ingress Control Plane Policing (installed by default) •.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the guide say about Ingress VLAN Policy with IPv4 and/or MAC classes?

Ingress VLAN Policy with IPv4 and/or MAC classes: Any unsupported feature may use more than one hardware entry to represent the range of L4 ports. On the 6200, 6300, 6400, 8100, 8360, 9300, and 9300S switch series, any ACE that uses It, gt, range, or a port group, may use more than one hardware entry to represent the range of L4 ports. L4 port ranges are not supported on these platforms. NOTE On 9300 Switch series, an ACE does not match L4 Ports with the SCTP protocol due to hardware limitations.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 route command do?

The IPv6 route command the key length for the plaintext value in the key pa rameter has been increased from 32 to 128 characte rs. The 10040 Switch Series now support the ability to creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Agg regation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines c onfiguration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. The 10040 Switch Series now support the ability to assign a persona LAG (Link Aggregation Group) to a LAG interface. The 10040 Switch Series now support the ability to show all interface personas and their attached inter faces. The name parameter is now supported on the 1004 0 Switch Series. This parameter assigns a name to th e destination route. Syntax: IPv6 route vrf.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the clear arp command do?

The clear arp command clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries from the ARP table. If you do not specify any VRF or port parameters, ARP table entries are cleared for the default VRF. Specifies a port on the switch. For example: 1/1/1. ip <A. B. C. D>|all] IPv6 <X: X: : X: X>|all vrf (Optional) Include an IP address to clear neighbor entries for th at specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries for all IP addresses. (Optional) Include an IPv6 address to clear neighbor entries for that specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries fo r all IPv6 addresses. Clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries for the specified VRF or for all VRFs. If no VRF is specified he default VRF is cleared. all-vrfs Clear neighbor entries for all VRFs Clear neighbor entries for the specified VRF. Syntax: clear arp.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip local-proxy-arp command do?

The ip local-proxy-arp command enables local proxy ARP on the specified interface. Local proxy ARP is supported on Layer 3 physical interfaces and on VLAN interfaces. To enable local proxy ARP on an interface, routing must be enabled on that interface. The no ip local-proxy-arp command disables local proxy ARP on the specified interface. uplink-passthrough (For 10000 Switch series only) Enables local proxy ARP passth rough to uplink members. NOTE When enabling ip local-proxy-arp uplink- passthrough, ip local-proxy-arp will be imp licitly enabled. Syntax:
- no ip local-proxy-arp
- ip local-proxy-arp 203.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip proxy-arp command do?

The ip proxy-arp command enables proxy ARP for the specified Layer 3 interface. Proxy ARP is supported on Layer 3 physical interfaces, LAG interfaces, and VLAN interfaces. It is disabled by default. To enable proxy ARP on an interface, routing must be enabled on that interface. The no form of this command disables proxy ARP for the specified interface. Syntax: no ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show arp timeout command do?

The show arp timeout command shows the age-out period for each ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) entry for a port, LAG, or VLAN interface. Specifies a physical port, VLAN, or LAG on the switch. For physi cal ports, use the format member/slot/port (for example, 1/3/ 1). Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. <INTERFACE> vsx-peer Shows the ARP table for all VRF instances, or for the named VRF. Specifies all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Length: 1 to 32 alphanumeric cha racters. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show arp timeout [<INTERFACE>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The bfd <IPv4-ADDR> command enables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. BFD is asynchronous and echo mode is supported. The no form of this command disables BFD under VRRP for the specified IP address. Public bfd <IPv4-ADDR> 249 Specifies the address on which to enable BFD in IPv4 format (x. x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no bfd <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd all-interfaces command do?

The bfd all-interfaces command enables BFD on all OSPFv2 or OSPFv3 interfaces. The no form of this command disables BFD on all active OSPFv2/OSPFv3 or IPv4/IPv6 interfaces, excluding those on which BFD was enabled at the interface level with the commands ip ospf bfd and IPv6 o spfv3 bfd. Syntax: no bfd all-interfaces.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd echo disable command do?

The bfd echo disable command disables support for BFD echo packets. Echo packet support is enabled by default. Public bfd echo disable 256 The no form of this command enables support for BFD echo packets. NOTE Toggling this feature on 8100, 8325, 8360, 9300, 9300S, 10040 switches may cause route flapping. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Syntax: no bfd echo disable.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD echo packets. The no form of this command removes the configured BFD echo packets interval. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. NOTE BFD IPv6 Echo is not supported. Specifies the minimum reception interval in milliseconds. A valu e of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD echo packets. Range: 0, 50 to 1000. Default: 500. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: no bfd min-echo-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command do?

The bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL> command sets the minimum time interval between received BFD control packets on an interface. The no form of this command removes the configured BFD minimum interval on an interface. If the interval is not set, the default interval is used. <INTERVAL> Specifies the minimum receive interval in milliseconds. A value of 0 means that the switch does not support reception of BFD c ontrol packets. Range: 100 to 20000. Default: 3000. Public bfd min-receive-interval 259. Syntax: no bfd min-receive-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the neighbor fall-over command do?

The neighbor fall-over command enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Enables BGP to register with BFD to receive fast peering session deactivation messages from BFD. You can either configure BFD support for BGP per neighbor or peer-group. The no form of this command disables BGP for BFD. NOTE Multihop BFD is not supported for BGP. <IPv4-ADDR> Specifies the neighbor address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf <VRF-NAME> command creates a VRF instance named <VRF-NAME> and then enters its context. Use default for <VRF-NAME> to enter the default VRF configure context. Except for the default VRF, the no form of the command deletes the named VRF instance and any IP configuration for interfaces or SVI linked to default VRF. The default VRF cannot be deleted and a warning is given if attempted. To erase the Route-Distinguisher and Route-Targets, enter the default VRF context and delete them manually one by one. Specifies the VRF name. Range: Up to 32 alphanumeric chara cters. The mgmt VRF cannot be used. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf <VRF-NAME>.

The IP address must be configured on the interface to enable BIDIR-PIM. Public BIDIR-PIM commands 462 Enables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Disables PIM Bidirectional on the interface. Syntax: ip pim-bidir [enable | disable].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE> command changes the maximum time, in seconds, before the router transmits the initial PIM hello message on the current interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets it to default. The default value is 5. Configures the given value as the hello interval. Range: <0-5>. <DELAY-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value> command changes the frequency at which the router transmits PIM hello messages on the current subnet. The router uses hello packets to inform neighbor routers of its presence and to comput the hello hold time. Hello hold time informs neighbor routers estimated wait time for the next hello packet from the router. Shortening the hello interval reduces the hello hold time. Default is set to 30 seconds. The no form of this command resets the frequency of PIM hello messages back to the default value on the current subnet. Public ip pim-bidir hello-interval 465 Configures the given value as hello interval. Range: 1-300 seco nds. • The default value (30 seconds) covers most deployment needs; change only for explicit convergence or timing requirements. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-interval <interval-value>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command do?

The ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value> command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. In chatty networks where there is a chance of Hello packets being lost, configuring a higher hold time reduces the likelihood of adjacency loss. Syntax: no ip pim-bidir hello-holdtime <holdtime-value>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command do?

The apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in command applies a policy to the current physical interface port or LAG or VLAN context. Public apply policy (config-if, config-lag-if, config-if-... 561 a p p l y p o l i c y (c o n fi g - i f, c o n fi g - l a g - i f, c o n fi g - i f -... Only one direction of a policy can be applied to an interface or VLAN at a time, thus using the apply command on an interface or VLAN with an already-applied policy of the same direction will replace the currently applied policy. NOTE The VLAN context supports the in and out directions, which apply to both bridged and routed traffic. The Interface VLAN context only supports the routed-in direction which applies only to routed traffic. The no form of this command removes a policy from the interface or VLANspecified by the current context. Syntax: no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} command do?

The apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in} command applies a policy to the global config context. Only one policy can be globally applied at a time. Applying a policy globally again, replaces the previous globally applied policy. The no form of this command removes application of the global policy. Public apply policy (config) 565 Specifies the policy to apply. in. Syntax: no apply policy <POLICY-NAME> {in}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command do?

The ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR> command configures a gateway address for the DHCP relay agent to use for DHCP requests. By default DHCP relay agent picks the lowest-numbered IP address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the gateway address. Specifies the IP address of the gateway in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: no ip bootp-gateway <IPv4-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command do?

The ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ] command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <DOMAIN-NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command do?

The ip dns host <HOST-NAME> command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. Public ip dns host 813 host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: no ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> command this command creates an ERPS ring with a given ID. The no form of this command removes all the configurations of the ring, including instances. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> This command configures the ERPS ring member port. An L2 interface in the switch is associated to one of the two member ports of an ERPS ring. In case of an interconnection node, only port0 is applicable for the sub-ring. The no form of this command removes the association of the ring port to the L2 interface on the switch. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <PORT0> Required, set port0 of the ring. Public erps ring <RINGID> <port0|port1> interface 836 Required, set port1 of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> hold-off-interval command specifies hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. If specified, a defect is not reported immediately. Instead, the hold-off timer is started. On expiration of the timer, if the defect still exists, it is reported to protection switching. The default value for hold-off timer is 0. The no form of this command removes the configured value of the hold-off interval and sets it to the default value of 0. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the hold-off interval in units of 100 ms. De fault: 0. <RINGID> <100 milliseconds> On a common ERPS network, a physical ring can be configured with a single ERPS ring, and only one blocked port can be specified in the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> control-vlan command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> control-vlan command this command adds a control-channel VLAN to a ring instance. In an ERPS ring, the control VLANshould be used only to forward RAPS PDUs and not service packets. All the devices in an ERPS ring instance must be configured with the same control VLAN, and different ERPS ring instances must use different control VLANs. The no form of this command removes the control-channel VLAN of the ring instance. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> <VID> This command adds descriptive information to help administrators and operators understand the purpose of a ring instance. 1-64 printable ASCII characters are allowed. The no form of this command removes the ring instance description. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> instance <ID> revertive command configures the default revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In revertive operation, after the conditions causing protection switching are cleared, traffic channels are restored to the recovered link blocking the RPL. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. The no form of this command configures non-revertive mode of operation for an ERPS ring. In non-revertive operation, the traffic channels continue to use the RPL, if it has not failed, after conditions causing protection switching are cleared. This configuration is meaningful only on the RPL node. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 <RINGID> <ID> In ERPS, there is a central node called RPL Owner Node which blocks one of the ports to ensure that there is no loop formed for the Ethernet traffic. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command do?

The erps ring <RINGID> meg-level command the R-APS messages transmitted by ERPS take the form of OAM PDUs as defined in G.8013. Each OAM PDU is transmitted at a specified level known as the Maintenance Entity Group (MEG) level. This command configures the level with which the ERPS packets must be transmitted. The no form of this command removes the configured MEG level and sets it to the default value of 7. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Range: 1-239 Required, specifies the meg-level. Range: 0-7. Default: 7. <RINGID> <0-7> This command associates a sub-ring to a parent-ring and is required for the sub-ring to notify the parent- ring on change in topology. The no form of this command removes the parent ring identifier. Required, specifies the ID of the ring. Syntax: erps ring <RINGID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the copy primary secondary command do?

The copy primary secondary command copies the firmware image from the primary to the secondary location. Downloads a hot-patch or firmware image from a TFTP or SFTP server. Public copy <REMOTE-URL> 985 <REMOTE-URL> TFTP format: Specifies the URL from which to download the firmware using S FTP or TFTP. tftp://<IP-ADDR>[: <PORT-NUM>] [; blocksize=<Value>]/<FILENAME> SFTP format: sftp://<USERNAME>@<IP-ADDR> [: <PORT-NUM>]/<FILENAME> SCP format: scp://USER@{IP|HOST}[: PORT]/FILE {hot-patch|primary| secondary} Select a hot-patch or a primary or secondary image profile for receiving the downloaded firmware. Required. NOTE For more information about hot-patch, see hot -patch. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Syntax: copy primary secondary.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the redundancy switchover command do?

The redundancy switchover command causes the switch to immediately switch over to the Standby Management Module. This command must be executed from the Active Management Module and will fail if the Standby Management Module is in a failed state or not present. Syntax: redundancy switchover.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip icmp unreachable command do?

The ip icmp unreachable command enables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages on the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. The unreachable host address originates from the failed packed. Default setting. The no form of this command disables the sending of ICMPv4 and ICMPv6 destination unreachable messages from the switch to a source host when a specific host is unreachable. This command does not prevent other hosts from sending an ICMP unreachable message. Syntax: no ip icmp unreachable.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the IGMP join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command unconfigures the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. Syntax: no ip igmp apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE> command configures an IGMP last member query interval value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the value to a default of 1 second on an interface. Specifies an IGMP last-member-query-interval on the inter face. Default: 1 second. Range: 1-2 seconds. <INTERVAL-VALUE>. Syntax: no ip igmp last-member-query-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command do?

The ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME> command configures initial IGMP querier-wait-time value in seconds. The no form of this command sets the IGMP querier-wait-time to the default value of 260 seconds. Note that the wait timer can be configured to any numbers within the 1-300 second range. Configures IGMP querier-wait-time to desired value. <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>. Syntax: no ip igmp querier-wait-time <QUERIER-WAIT-TIME>.

The ip igmp querier query-max-response-time <RESPONSE-TIME> command configures the IGMP querier max response time value in seconds on an interface, depending on the command context you are in. The no form of this command sets the querier max response time value to the default of 10 seconds on an interface. Specifies the IGMP querier max response time value on the inte rface. messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command do?

The ip igmp robustness <VALUE> command configures IGMP robustness on an interface, depending on the command context. The robustness parameter allows tuning for the expected packet loss on a subnet. The no form of this command sets the robustness value to the default of 2 on an interface. Specifies an IGMP robustness value on the interface. Default: 2. Range: 1-7. Public ip igmp robustness 1067. Syntax: no ip igmp robustness <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp router-alert-check command do?

The ip igmp router-alert-check command enables or disables IGMP router alert check for IGMP packets. IGMP packets without the router alert field set are dropped if router alert check is enabled. Router alert check is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables router alert check for IGMP packets. Enable IGMP router alert check. Disable IGMP router alert check. Syntax: ip igmp router-alert-check [enable | disable].

You can configure a maximum of 32 IGMP static groups. The no form of the command unconfigures IGMP static multicast group on an interface. Specifies an IGMP static multicast group IP address on the inter face. Format: A. B. C. D <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>. Syntax: no ip igmp static-group <MULTICAST-GROUP-IP>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the class command do?

The class command creates a new IFA class for IPv4 or IPv6 traffic. The no form of this command removes an IFA class for IPv4 or IPv6 traffic. Specifies the IFA based Internet Protocol v4 (IFA-IPv4). Specifies the IFA based Internet Protocol v6 (IFA-IPv6). Specifies the IFA class name. Configures an IFA IPv4 or IPv6 Flow Filter Class based on 5-tuple. The IFA Flow Filter can filter by protocols UDP and TCP, source IP, source port, destination IP, and destination port. The no form of this command removes the IFA Flow Filter Class based on the entry sequence number. Specifies a sequence number for the match number. Range: 1 to 4294967295. <sequence_number> udp tcp <src_ip_address> <prefix_length> <subnet_mask> <dst_ip_address> any eq gt lt Specifies the user datagram protocol. Syntax: class {ifa-ip | ifa-IPv6} <NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command do?

The ip igmp snooping all-vlans command enable IGMP snooping on all VLANs. This configuration setting enables IGMP snooping on all existing VLANs, as well as on any VLANs that are created after the ip igmp snooping all-vlans command is issued. When IGMP snooping is enabled globally, IGMP snooping can not be configured on individual VLANs within the VLAN context of the command-line interface. The no form of this command disables global IGMP snooping, allows IGMP snooping to remain enabled at the VLAN level, and allows the configuration of VLAN-level IGMP snooping settings. Syntax: no ip igmp snooping all-vlans.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface loopback <ID> command do?

The interface loopback <ID> command creates a loopback interface and changes to the config-loopback-if context. Loopback interfaces are layer 3. The no form of this command deletes a loopback interface. Specifies the loopback interface ID. Range: 1 to 256 <INSTANCE> Public interface loopback 1174. Syntax: no interface loopback <ID>.

The specified VLAN must already be defined on the switch. The no form of this command deletes an interface VLAN. Public interface vlan 1175 Specifies the loopback interface ID. <VLAN-ID> none. messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip tcp mss <VALUE> command do?

The ip tcp mss <VALUE> command sets the TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) MSS (Maximum Segment Size) for an interface. When configured, the MSS option in the TCP header is set in the TCP handshake for negotiation during connection establishment. Once the TCP session is established, changing the TCP MSS value on the interface will not affect the existing session. The no form of this command removes the TCP MSS configuration from the interface. NOTE TCP MSS is supported on ROP, SVI, subinterface, and L3 GRE tunnel interface. Specifies the IPv4 TCP MSS in bytes. Range: 68 to 65495. <VALUE>. Syntax: no ip tcp mss <VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip neighbor-flood command do?

The ip neighbor-flood command enables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface when a neighbor link goes down. The no form of this command disables VLAN flooding for the specified VLAN interface. Displays the status of IP Neighbor Flood on the specified interface along with other interface-related attributes. Specifies the interface name (for example, vlan30). Optional. <IFNAME> Displays the interfaces on which IP Neighbor Flood is enabled. Displays the current running configuration. Syntax: ip neighbor-flood.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command do?

The ip-sla <IP-SLA-NAME> command creates an IP Service Level Agreement (SLA) profile and switches to the config-ip-sla context. The no form of this command deletes an IP-SLA profile. By default, all profile use the default VRF (default). Specifies an IP-SLA profile name. Length: 1 to 64 characters. <IP-SLA-NAME> Selects the IP-SLA responder. The responder can be configured for udp-echo, tcp-connect, udp-jitter-voip type. It requires the SLA name, SLA type, and port number as arguments. The no form of this command removes the IP-SLA responder. Specifies the IP-SLA responder name. Length: 1 to 64 chara cters. <SLA-NAME> udp-echo tcp-connect Enables responder for udp-echo probes. Selects TCP connect as the IP-SLA test mechanism. udp-jitter-voip Selects VOIP jitter as the IP-SLA test mechanism. Syntax: ip-sla responder <SLA-NAME> (udp-echo | tcp-connect | udp-jitter-voip).

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> command sets the local IP address of a GRE tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to a GRE tunnel. Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading ze ros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192. 168.5.100. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the vrf attach <VRF-NAME> command do?

The vrf attach <VRF-NAME> command assigns an IP tunnel to a VRF. By default, all tunnels are automatically assigned to the default VRF when they are created. The no form of this command assigns a tunnel to the default VRF (default). Specifies the VRF name to which to assign the tunnel. <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: no vrf attach <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command do?

The ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred command enables the IPv6-only-preferred mode on the VLAN interface, and is also supported on the OOBM interface. Once enabled, the DHCP client prioritizes a IPv6 address over an IPv4 address. To obtain a DHCPv4 address, the IPv6-only-preferred command must be disabled, and the DHCPv4 client must be restarted. This command is disabled by default. NOTE Ensure the server supports the IPv6-only-preferred mode option, as the command will not work unless supported by the server. The no form of this command disables IPv6-only-preferred mode. NOTE The IPv6-only-preferred mode command must be configured prior to the ip dhcp command, otherwise it will not take effect. Syntax: no ip dhcp option IPv6-only-preferred.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command do?

The IPv6 address <global-unicast-address> command sets a global unicast address on the interface. The no form of this command removes the global unicast address on the interface. NOTE This command automatically creates an IPv6 link-local address on the interface. However, it does not add the IPv6 address link-local command to the running configuration. If you remove the IPv6 address, the link-local address is also removed. To maintain the link-local address, you must manually execute the IPv6 address link-local command. Syntax: no IPv6 address <global-unicast-address>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp holdtime <TIME> command specifies the maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives. When a new advertisement arrives, hold time is reset. Hold time must be greater than or equal to the maximum advertisement interval. Therefore, if the hold time for an advertisement expires, the host can reasonably conclude that the router interface that sent the advertisement is no longer available. The default hold time is three times the maximum advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum amount of time the host will consider an advertisement to be valid until a newer advertisement arrives and update it to the default value. Specifies the lifetime of router advertisements sent from this in terface. Range: 4 to 9000 seconds. Syntax: no ip irdp holdtime <TIME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the maximum router advertisement interval. The no form of this command removes the specified maximum router advertisement interval and reverts to the default value. Public ip irdp maxadvertinterval 1379 Specifies the maximum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 4 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 600 seconds. <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp maxadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command do?

The ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME> command specifies the minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements. By default, this value is automatically set by the switch to be 75% of the value configured for maximum router advertisement interval. Use this command to override the automatically configured value. The no form of this command removes the specified minimum amount of time the switch waits between sending router advertisements and reverts to the default value. Specifies the minimum time allowed between the sending of u nsolicited router advertisements. Range: 3 to 1800 seconds. De fault: 450 seconds (75% of the default value for maximum route r advertisement interval). <TIME>. Syntax: no ip irdp minadvertinterval <TIME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the interface persona lag <NAME> command do?

The interface persona lag <NAME> command creates a persona LAG, a template for LAG (Link Aggregation Group) interfaces. A persona LAG defines configuration options that can be applied to multiple LAGs for consistency and simplified management. Only LAG interfaces can reference persona LAG templates. The persona LAG name cannot use reserved interface names or formats, such as vlan, lag, or loopback. Configurations defined in a persona LAG are valid only for LAG interfaces. The no form of this command deletes the persona LAG interface. Specifies the name of the persona LAG. <NAME>. Syntax: no interface persona lag <NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command do?

The ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary] command sets an IPv4 address and subnet mask to a LAG interface. One primary and up to 31 secondary address can be configured per interface. The no form of this command removes the IPv4 address from the interface. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.1 00. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Specifies a secondary IP address. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> secondary. Syntax: no ip address <IPv4-ADDR>/<MASK> [secondary].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command do?

The aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators command enables/disables privilege elevation for the users in an administrators group. When enabled, an administrative user is provided lower privilege access upon successful switch login. The user must execute an enable command and re-authenticate to elevate their privilege to administrator (the administrator level Public aaa authentication login privilege-elevation 1558 access with ‘#’ prompt). Executing the disable command revokes an administrator privilege and returns the user to the lower privilege access level (the operator level access ‘>’ prompt). The no form of this command disables privilege elevation for the administrator user group. Syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators.

command: aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators
syntax: no aaa authentication login privilege-elevation group administrators

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command do?

The aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST> command defines authorization as being basic local RBAC (specified as none), or as full-fledged local RBAC specified as local (the default), or as remote TACACS+ (specified with group <GROUP-LIST>). Each available connection type (channel) can be configured individually. All server groups named in the command, must exist. This command can be issued multiple times, once for each connection type. The no form of this command unconfigures authorization for the specified connection type, reverting to the default of local. NOTE Although only TACACS+ servers are supported for remote authorization, local authorization (basic or full-fledged) can be used with remote RADIUS authentication. Syntax: no aaa authorization commands <CONNECTION-TYPE> group <GROUP-LIST>.

The show mka statistics command shows MKA statistics for all MAC sec-enabled interfaces or a specific interface or interface range. The MKA statistics are refreshed periodically, approximately every five seconds. interface <IF-RANGE> Specifies one or more interfaces for which MKA statistics inform ation is to be shown. Shows MKA status information for all MAC sec-enabled interfaces or a specific interface or interface range. interface <IF-RANGE> Specifies one or more interfaces for which MKA status informati on is to be shown. Configures the Ether-Type for use in frames for MKA. The no form of the command uses the default EAPoL ether-type 0x888e for MKA. Syntax: show mka statistics [interface <IF-RANGE>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping command this command configures the drop unknown mode. While MLD snooping is enabled, the traffic will be forwarded only to ports that initiate an MLD request for multicast. Drop unknown mode can be a filter across all VLANs (vlan-shared) or per VLAN (exclusive-vlan). The default configuration is vlan-shared. The no form of this command configures the drop unknown mode on the switch to the default vlan-shared. Required: Enable shared VLAN filter on the switch. vlan-exclusive Required: Enable exclusive drop unknown filter per VLAN. This command disables or reenables MLD snooping on the VLAN. Starting with AOS-CX 10.16, MLD snooping is enabled by default. Public MLD snooping VLAN configuration commands 1728 The no form of this command disables all MLD snooping configurations on the VLAN. Syntax: IPv6 mld snooping drop-unknown {vlan-shared | vlan-exclusive}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures the ACL on a particular interface to filter the MLD join or leave packets based on rules set in the particular ACL name. The no form of this command disables the rules set for the ACL. NOTE This configuration will override the ACL associated with IGMP snooping on the corresponding L2 VLAN. Associates an ACL with the IGMP. Specifies the name of the ACL. <ACL-NAME> NOTE If the access list is configured for both L2 VLA N and L3 VLAN, the L3 VLAN configuration will be applied. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping apply access-list <ACL-NAME>

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command this command configures the given ports in auto mode, which is the default port mode. The no form of this command disables auto ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as an auto port. Specifies the number of a single VL AN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by c ommas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [auto vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command by default ports are configured in auto mode. This command configures the given ports in blocked mode. The no form of this command removes blocked ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a blocked port. Specifies the number of a single V Public IPv6 mld snooping blocked vlan 1734 LAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 6 0). Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [blocked vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the specified ports as fastleave ports. Enables the switch to immediately remove an interface from the bridge table upon receiving the leave group message. The no form of this command disables fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, separated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (10-40, 60). MLD fastleave is configured for ports on a per-VLAN basis. By default, the querier sends a MLD Group- Specific Query message out of the interface, upon which the leave group message is received to ensure that no other receivers are connected to the interface. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [fastleave vlan <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>] command configures the given ports in forced fastleave mode. The no form of this command disables forced fastleave configuration on the ports. Required: Specifies a list of VLANs on which the port should be configured as a forced fastleave port. Specifies the number of a single VLAN or a series of numbers for a range of VLANs, sepa Public IPv6 mld snooping forced fastleave vlan 1738 rated by commas (10, 20, 30, 40), dashes (10-40), or both (1 0-40, 60). With forced fastleave enabled, MLD speeds up the process of blocking unnecessary multicast traffic to a switch port that is connected to multiple end nodes. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping [forced-fastleave <VLAN-LIST>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command do?

The IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all command this command enables AOS-CX leaf devices (access-layer switches that connect directly to servers, storage, and other endpoints) to unconditionally forward multicast traffic to all VTEPs that do not support the exchange of MLD reports over the fabric. By default, when MLD snooping is enabled in AOS-CX, multicast traffic is selectively forwarded only to VTEPs connected to have interested hosts. This is achieved by exhanging MLD reports over VXLAN tunnels where each VTEP processes the remote reports and uses the membership information to forward multicast traffic efficiently, conserving fabric bandwidth. Syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all.

syntax: no IPv6 mld snooping vxlan replicate-all

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show spanning-tree inconsistent-ports (MSTP) command do?

The show spanning-tree inconsistent-ports (MSTP) command shows ports blocked by STP protection functions such as Root guard, Loop guard, BPDU guard, and RPVST guard in addition to MSTI information. Specifies the MSTP instance ID. Range: 1 to 4094. <INSTANCE-ID> Shows MSTP configuration and status information for each instance. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show spanning-tree inconsistent-ports [instance <INSTANCE-ID>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command do?

The ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include command enables the router to forward multicast data received on the VXLAN L3VNI fabric to this interface, regardless of whether a multicast join was received on this interface or not. This allows the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. This command must be enabled on the VSX VXLAN routers to support multicast receivers over ROP or P2P SVI extensions. This command can be enabled on a transit L3 peering between VSX peers. This command is optional when the uplink is an MCLAGsVI. The no form of the command disables forwarding of multicast data on the interface. NOTE • An IP address must be configured on the interface and pim-sparse must be enabled. • This command must be enabled only on one interface per VRF. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse datapath-auto-include.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor command do?

The ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor command once configured, the router processes IGMP/MLD and PIM joins received on this interface regardless of its DR or Prime Neighbor role. The command must be enabled for VSX VXLAN leaf switches for both L2 and L3 extensions. This allows for the interface to be in the same multicast data path state on both the VSX peers. The no form of the command disables the vsx-virtual-neighbor on the interface. NOTE This command is applicable for normal SVI interfaces and L2 VNI mapped SVI interfaces. It is valid for VXLAN-enabled VLANs only and has no effect on non-VXLAN-enabled VLANs. Syntax: no ip pim-sparse vsx-virtual-neighbor.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 mroute command do?

The show IPv6 mroute command shows multicast routing information. Optionally, you can show specific information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows all PIM neighbors information. vrf <VRF-NAME> Shows PIM neighbor information for a specific VRF. vsx-peer Displays the IPv6 information about PIM6 interfaces currently configured in the router for the given VLAN. If the VLAN name is not specified, the output of this command displays information for the default VLAN. Specifies the VLAN. Displays the information about PIM interfaces currently configured in the router for the given VRF. If VRF is not given, it displays for default VRF. NOTE The overlay IPv6 address of the LRVNI is an autogenerated link local IP that is derived by PIM using the VXLAN source IP. Syntax: show IPv6 mroute [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp enable command do?

The ntp enable command enables the NTP client on the switch to automatically adjust the local time and date on the switch. The NTP client is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables the NTP client. Syntax: no ntp enable.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command do?

The ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID> command sets a key as trusted. When NTP authentication is enabled, the switch only synchronizes with time servers that transmit packets containing a trusted key. The no form of this command removes the trusted designation from a key. Specifies the identification number of the key to set as trusted. Range: 1 to 65534. <KEY-ID>. Syntax: no ntp trusted-key <KEY-ID>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf dead-interval command do?

The ip ospf dead-interval command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead if no hello packet is received on the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPF interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally 4 times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL> Sets the time interval between OSPF hello packets for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the time interval OSPF hello packets to the default of 10 seconds for the OSPF interface. Specifies the time interval for the hello interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 10. <INTERVAL> Sets the key chain for md5 authentication. Syntax: ip ospf dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command do?

The ip ospf message-digest-key md5 command sets the md5 message digest authentication key. If the md5 key is given in ciphertext, it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. The no form of this command deletes the md5 authentication key. Specifies the md5 key ID. Range: 1 to 255. <KEY-ID> {ciphertext | plaintext} Selects the md5 key format. Specifies the md5 authentication key. <KEY> NOTE When the authentication key is not provided on the command line, plaintext key prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered key characters are masked with asterisks. Configures the OSPF network type for the interface. Public ip ospf network 2065 The no form of this command sets the network type for the interface to the system default which is broadcast network. Syntax: ip ospf message-digest-key <KEY-ID> md5 [{ciphertext | plaintext} <KEY>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip ospf lsdb command do?

The show ip ospf lsdb command shows the OSPF link state database summary for different OSPF LSAs (Link State Advertisement). Use the parameters to get information for a particular LSA. Enter an OSPF process ID to display general OSPF information for a particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> adv-router {<ROUTER-ID>| self} Select to display link states for a particular advertising router. S pecify either a Router ID of the advertising router or specify sel f to show self-originated link states. area <AREA-ID> Select to display information filtered for the specified area in on e of the following formats. • OSPF area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a d ecimal number from 0 to 255. • OSPF area identifier in decimal format. Value: 0 to 4294967 295. Syntax: show ip ospf [<process-id>] lsdb.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip ospf routes command do?

The show ip ospf routes command displays OSPF routing information. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> protected Specify an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a deci mal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display neighbor information for all VRFs. Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Selects a protected route. Specifying an IPv4 address will select protected route for that prefix. Displays OSPF statistics. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] routes.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip ospf statistics interface command do?

The show ip ospf statistics interface command displays OSPF statistics for the OSPF-enabled interfaces. <PROCESS-ID> <INTERFACE-NAME> all-vrfs Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics information on the specified OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display information only for the specified interface. Select to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics informatio n for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Displays the current state and parameters of the OSPF virtual links. <PROCESS-ID> brief all-vrfs Enter an OSPF process ID to display information on the OSPF vi rtual links for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display brief overview information for the OSPF virtual links. Select to display OSPF virtual links information for all VRFs. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>} command configures a static OSPFv3 neighbor on the interface for point-to-multipoint networks. Static neighbors are required on non-broadcast networks where automatic neighbor discovery using multicast hello packets is not possible. The neighbor IPv6 address must be the link-local IPv6 address of the neighboring router on the same link. This command is valid only on interfaces configured with the point-to-multipoint network type. It is required for non-broadcast point-to-multipoint networks. The no version of this command removes the static neighbor configuration from the interface. neighbor <neighbor-IPv6- Static neighbor IPv6 link-local address. address>. Syntax: no IPv6 ospfv3 {neighbor <neighbor-IPv6-address>}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID> command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. Public IPv6 ospfv3 area 2172 The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <AREA-ID> <PROCESS-ID> Configures null authentication on an interface which disables IPsec authentication. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication null.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 cost command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 cost command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface. The interface cost is used as a parameter to calculate the best routes. The no form of this command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface to the default of 1. Specifies the interface cost value. Range: 1 to 65535. Default: 1. <INTERFACE-COST> Sets the interval after a neighbor is declared dead when no hello packet is received on the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPFv3 interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally four times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL> Configures IPsec ESP authentication. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 cost <INTERFACE-COST>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 network command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 network command configures the network type for the interface. By default, the network type is broadcast. The no form of this command sets the network type for the interface to the system default of broadcast network. Specifies the OSPFv3 network type as a broadcast multiaccess network. Specifies the OSPFv3 network type as a point-to-point netw ork. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 network {broadcast|point-to-point}.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command do?

The ip pim-dense hello-holdtime command configures the Hello Hold time (in seconds) advertised in PIM Hello messages on this interface; this overrides the default value derived from the current hello-interval. By default, the hold time that a neighbor uses before declaring this router down is automatically calculated (typically a multiple of the configured hello-interval). Manually configuring the hello-holdtime allows finer control of neighbor timeout detection. A smaller value can speed convergence (neighbors age out faster if hellos stop) but increases sensitivity to transient loss; a larger value provides stability at the cost of slower failure detection. If not configured, the implementation derives the value from the current hello-interval. Syntax: ip pim-dense hello-holdtime <holdtime-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip mroute group-addr command do?

The show ip mroute group-addr command shows the multicast routing information for the given group address. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <GROUP-ADDR> <SOURCE-ADDR> Specifies a group address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Specifies show information for the group from this source in IP v 4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. all-vrfs Shows mroute information for the group for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. vsx-peer Shows brief version of the multicast routing information. Optionally, you can specify the display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Shows mroute information for all VRFs. Syntax: show ip mroute <GROUP-ADDR> [<SOURCE-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <vrf-name>].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip pim rpf (PIM-DM) command do?

The show ip pim rpf (PIM-DM) command displays PIM RPF details for the specified source or RP address in the given VRF and shows the nexthop and interface through which the shortest path to the source is available. It also displays if a PIM neighbor is present on the nexthop. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <IP-ADDRESS> decimal number from 0 to 255. (Optional) Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), whe re x is a all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Shows PIM RPF details for the given IP address. (Optional) Shows PIM interface information for all VRFs. (Optional) Shows PIM interface information for a particular VRF. If the <VRF-NAME> is not specified, it shows the default VRF information. Syntax: show ip pim rpf [<IP-ADDRESS>][all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay command configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Default: 500 ms. R ange: 250-2000. <DELAY-VALUE> Configures the multicast datagram time-to-live (router hop-count) threshold for the interface. Any IP multicast datagrams or state-refresh packets with a TTL less than this threshold will not be forwarded out the interface. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets to the default of 3 attempts. Specifies the time-to-live threshold. Default: 3 attempts. Ran ge: 0-255. <THRESHOLD-VALUE> Removes PIM-DM for all IPv6 related configurations for the interface. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-dense propagation-delay <DELAY-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show IPv6 pim6 rpf (PIM-DM) command do?

The show IPv6 pim6 rpf (PIM-DM) command displays PIM RPF details for the specified source or RP address in the given VRF and shows the nexthop and interface through which the shortest path to the source is available. It also displays if a PIM neighbor is present on the nexthop. Optionally, you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. <IP-ADDRESS> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Optional. Specifies an IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxx x: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Shows PIM RPF details for the given IP address. Optional. Shows PIM interface information for all VRFs. Optional. Shows PIM interface information for a particular VRF. I f the <VRF-NAME> is not specified, it shows the default VRF i nformation. Syntax: show IPv6 pim6 rpf [<IP-ADDRESS>][all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show ip pim pending command do?

The show ip pim pending command shows the pending joins on a PIM router. Optionally you can specify display information by VRF. If no options are specified, it shows information for the default VRF. Use this command to determine what flows are being requested on the PIM network. If data availability for a flow is expected, and a join for the flow is pending, the troubleshooting search moves to the source of that flow, since the routers are verified to be seeing the request for data. Specifies a group address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. <GROUP-ADDR> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> vsx-peer Selects all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. Syntax: show ip pim pending [<GROUP-ADDR>] [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay command enables the LAN prune delay option on the current interface. The default is enabled. With LAN-prune-delay enabled, the router informs downstream neighbors how long it will wait before pruning a flow after receiving a prune request. Other downstream routers on the same interface must send a join to override the prune before the LAN-prune-delay time to continue the flow. Prompts any downstream neighbors with multicast receivers continuing to belong to the flow to reply with a join. If no joins are received after the LAN-prune-delay period, the router prunes the flow. The propagation-delay and override-interval settings determine the LAN-prune-delay setting. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay 2501 The no form of this command disables the LAN prune delay option. Syntax: no IPv6 pim6-sparse lan-prune-delay.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval command do?

The IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval command configures the override interval that gets inserted into the Override Interval field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes the currently configured value and sets the value to the default of 2500 ms. Specifies the override interval of a LAN Prune Delay option in m s. Range: 500 to 6000. Default: 2500. <INTERVAL-VALUE> Configures the propagation delay that gets inserted into the LAN prune delay field of a LAN Prune Delay option. The no form of this command removes currently configured value and sets to the default of 500 ms. Specifies the propagation delay value in ms. Range: 250 to 200 0. Default: 500. Public IPv6 pim6-sparse propagation-delay 2504 Configures the frequency at which the router will send periodic join or prune-interval messages. Syntax: IPv6 pim6-sparse override-interval <INTERVAL-VALUE>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME> command configures an application to associate with a user-configured certificate. By default, all applications are associated with the default certificate "local-cert". Note that applications "captive-portal" and "hsc" are only available on certain platforms. The certificate associated with EST client is global to all EST profiles. Among the available applications, only dot1x-supplicant, est-client, and radsec-client are allowed to associate with device-identity certificate. Also note that device-identity certificate on a switch using a TPM of legacy version 1.2 only works with TLS version 1.2. 1. Syntax: no crypto pki application <APP-NAME> certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command do?

The crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME> command creates a leaf certificate and changes to its context config-cert-<CERT-NAME>. If the specified leaf certificate exists, this command changes to its context. The first time the switch starts it creates a self-signed, default leaf certificate called local-cert. This certificate is used by any switch application that does not have an associated leaf certificate. The no form of this command deletes the specified leaf certificate. The default leaf certificate local-cert cannot be deleted. Specifies the name of a leaf certificate. Range: 1 to 32 alphanu meric characters (excluding "). <CERT-NAME>. Syntax: no crypto pki certificate <CERT-NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the qos queue-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos queue-profile <NAME> command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has one to eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: no qos queue-profile <NAME>.

source_file: 9300/10_17_1000/Command-line interface Reference Guide For 9300S and 10040 Switch Series.md

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the qos threshold-profile <NAME> command do?

The qos threshold-profile <NAME> command creates a QoS threshold profile and switches to the config-threshold context for the profile. If the specified threshold profile exists, this command switches to the config-threshold context for the existing profile. The threshold profile determines the action to take when a threshold is exceeded for each queue. A threshold profile is composed of up to 8 queues, numbered from 0 to 7. Each queue defines the action to take when buffer utilization exceeds a specific threshold. Configure queues with the command queue. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS threshold profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied to an interface can be removed. <NAME> Specifies the name of the QoS threshold profile to create or co nfigure. Syntax: no qos threshold-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 9300 AOS-CX 10.17.1000, what does the show queue-monitor status command do?

The show queue-monitor status command displays the global state of the queue monitoring feature. The output includes the global enable/disable status, the memory consumed by the feature, the statistics being collected, and the interfaces currently enabled for monitoring. queue-monitor This command enables the queue monitoring feature, allowing the switch to coll ect queue statistics at 10-second time intervals. These aggregate statistics ca n be used to monitor network health, troubleshoot network issues, and identify normal network behavior patterns and performance anomalies. This feature is di sabled by default, but can be enabled on a maximum of 52 interfaces per switch. Syntax: show queue-monitor status.
